entries.title: From Rough Draft to Real Product
entries.rawOfficialText:
Products can have rough drafts too. To see how well they work and what people think of them, companies often create early samples-or prototypes-of potential products. Here are some examples of prototypes that turned into popular gadgets and gizmos (aplenty); sometimes, as in the case of the iPhone, they may look nothing like the product that ultimately made it to market. Learn more about the prototyping cycle by researching the terms below, then discuss with your team: are there other things in life that would benefit from prototyping? Is there any difference between a prototype and a draft?
sketches | storyboarding | paper prototypes | low vs. high fidelity
wireframing | mockup | proof of concept | user testing
minimum viable product | minimum marketable feature
entries.studentExplanation:
Products often begin as rough drafts. Before a company builds something at full scale, it can test early versions to see what works, what fails, and what users actually need. A prototype may look unfinished, but that is its value: it lets people learn before they spend too much time, money, or trust on the final version.

The extra notes define the ladder of prototyping: sketches, storyboards, paper prototypes, wireframes, mockups, proof of concept, minimum viable product, and user testing are not synonyms. Each answers a different question: can we imagine it, can people understand it, can it work, and is it worth building? Use this to judge readiness: what kind of model is being shown, how close it is to the final thing, where humans or assumptions are hidden, and why a prototype can both teach and mislead.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this shows that a prototype is not a failed product but a learning version. At the section level, it opens Monkey See, Monkey Prototype by showing that invention usually moves through trial versions. At the year-theme level, it connects to progress because getting “there” often requires unfinished forms first.
entries.takeaway: Testing early versions can improve product decisions before the final release.
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether iteration is a sign of weakness or the most realistic way to move from idea to strong final product.
entries.counterargument: Iteration helps, but some fields lose meaning if everything is treated like a testable product waiting to be optimized.

items.title: The original iPhone prototype
items.note: Even one of the most influential modern products began in a form that looked technical, awkward, and very different from the final iPhone.
items.imageGuidance: The agent must use the IMAGESEARCH skill first to find a real image of the original iPhone prototype or Apple's early iPhone development hardware. This is a known object/case, so the agent must not use a generic phone mockup or generated image unless no real usable source exists. Prefer reputable tech journalism, museum/archive-style sources, or credible Apple-history sources. Preserve the raw-content link about the original iPhone prototype in the app.
items.videoGuidance: The agent must use the YOUTUBESEARCH skill to find an educational video of maximum 5-7 minutes explaining the early iPhone prototype or the product-development process behind the first iPhone. Avoid long keynotes unless a short extracted educational explanation is available.

items.title: Sketches
items.note: Sketches is presented as quick early drawings that help inventors and designers explore ideas before building anything.
items.imageGuidance: The agent must use the IMAGESEARCH skill first to find a real image of product-design sketches, ideally showing several rough design options on paper. The image should look like early thinking, not a finished advertisement. If no perfect image exists, the agent may use image generation to create a World Scholar app-style sketch sheet with rough product ideas, arrows, and notes.

items.title: Storyboarding
items.note: Storyboarding is presented as a way to map a sequence of actions, showing how a user might experience a product or service step by step.
items.imageGuidance: The agent must use the IMAGESEARCH skill first to find a UX storyboard or product-storyboard example with multiple panels showing a user journey. Avoid film storyboards unless they clearly explain sequence and user experience. If no suitable image exists, the agent may use image generation to create a World Scholar app-style storyboard with a student using a prototype across several panels.

items.youtubeSearchQuery: Storyboarding a clear educational video of maximum 5-7 minutes explaining UX storyboarding or product storyboarding.

items.title: Paper prototypes
items.note: Paper prototypes is presented as cheap, fast, physical mockups used to test layouts and interactions before coding or manufacturing.
items.imageGuidance: The agent must use the IMAGESEARCH skill first to find a real image of a paper prototype for an app, website, interface, or product. The image should clearly show hand-drawn screens, movable paper parts, buttons, or interaction areas. Prefer Wikimedia Commons or educational UX sources. If no perfect image exists, the agent may generate a World Scholar app-style image of paper app screens being tested by students.
items.videoGuidance: The agent must use the YOUTUBESEARCH skill to find a short educational video of maximum 5-7 minutes showing how paper prototyping works.
items.imageSearchQuery: Paper prototypes a real image of a paper prototype for an app, website, interface, or product. The image should clearly show hand-drawn screens, movable paper parts, buttons, or interaction areas. Prefer Wikimedia Common
items.youtubeSearchQuery: Paper prototypes a short educational video of maximum 5-7 minutes showing how paper prototyping works.

items.title: Low vs. high fidelity
items.note: Difference between rough exploratory prototypes and polished prototypes that look closer to the final product. is explained.
items.imageGuidance: The agent must use the IMAGESEARCH skill first to find a side-by-side comparison of low-fidelity and high-fidelity prototypes. The image should make the contrast obvious: rough blocks or sketches on one side, polished interface or product mockup on the other. If no perfect comparison exists, the agent may use image generation to create a clear World Scholar app-style split-screen comparison.

items.title: Mockup
items.note: Mockups is presented as more polished visual models that show what a product or interface might look like before it is fully functional.
items.imageGuidance: The agent must use the IMAGESEARCH skill first to find a product or app mockup that clearly looks more polished than a wireframe but not necessarily fully working. A phone-screen mockup, website mockup, or product-packaging mockup would work. If no good image exists, the agent may generate a World Scholar app-style mockup image.

items.title: User testing
items.note: User testing is presented as watching real people try a product so designers can learn what works, what confuses users, and what needs improvement.

Avoid generic office meeting images. If no strong image exists, the agent may generate a World Scholar app-style classroom user-testing scene.

items.title: Minimum viable product
items.note: MVP is presented as the smallest useful version of a product that can deliver value and teach the team what to improve next.

If no good image exists, the agent may generate a World Scholar app-style diagram showing a simple first version growing through feedback.

items.title: Minimum marketable feature
items.note: Minimum marketable feature is presented as a small but polished feature that is complete enough to release and matter to users.

entries.title: Fast Models, Real Risks
entries.rawOfficialText: Instead of hiring engineers to work with a factory in Shenzhen (or Baja California) to create a product sample, today an inventor can try out a new concept with simulated models and 3D printed mockups. Even your school might have a “makerspace” that you can use to channel your inner Thomas Edison-or Sarah Boone. Consider the advantages and disadvantages of such rapid prototyping, then discuss with your team: should access to these tools be limited to those who can use them responsibly?

links.label: Sarah Boone inventor profile or short video

entries.studentExplanation:
Rapid prototyping lets inventors test ideas much faster than before. Simulations, makerspaces, and 3D printers can make design more accessible, especially for students or small creators. But faster access also creates a responsibility problem: powerful tools can help people learn and build, but they can also be misused if people lack training, safety rules, or ethical judgment.

Add Sarah Boone as a concrete “prototype solves a real problem” case: the improved ironing board worked because it matched fitted sleeves and garments better than a flat plank. Rapid models, makerspaces, and 3D printing are powerful when they reduce risk early, but they can also hide safety, access, cost, and quality problems. Use this to judge readiness: what kind of model is being shown, how close it is to the final thing, where humans or assumptions are hidden, and why a prototype can both teach and mislead.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students weigh the benefits and risks of fast creation tools. At the section level, it shows that prototyping is also about access and responsibility. At the year-theme level, it connects to thresholds because easier tools can move an idea closer to real-world use very quickly.
entries.takeaway: Rapid prototyping expands access, but powerful tools still need safety and responsibility.
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating how open powerful building tools should be, and whether access and responsibility can grow together.
entries.counterargument: Stronger limits can reduce harm, but they can also turn creativity and experimentation into privileges for a narrow group.

items.title: Makerspaces
items.note: Makerspaces is presented as shared places where students or inventors can build, test, repair, and experiment with tools.
items.imageGuidance: The agent must use the IMAGESEARCH skill first to find a school or community makerspace image showing tools such as 3D printers, laser cutters, electronics, workbenches, or student projects. Avoid generic classrooms without maker tools. If no perfect image exists, the agent may generate a World Scholar app-style makerspace with students safely testing prototypes.

Preserve any Sarah Boone link in the app.

Avoid accident images or fear-based visuals. If no strong image exists, the agent may generate a World Scholar app-style image of students using maker tools responsibly with safety signs and supervision.

entries.title: Vibe Coding and the Flood of New Software
entries.rawOfficialText: In the world of software, AI-powered vibe coding allows users to create programs without writing a line of code. Just ask a chatbot for what you want and watch it appear. You might want to try vibe-coding yourself, then discuss with your team: should we be worried about a future flooded with too many programs of uncertain quality and with limited support? Is it better if not everyone can get “there” so easily?
entries.studentExplanation:
Vibe coding means using AI tools to create software from natural-language instructions instead of writing every line by hand. This can help beginners build quickly and make software creation feel more open. But it also creates a quality problem: if many people can generate apps without understanding the code, the result may be buggy, insecure, unsupported, or hard to maintain.

The sources frame vibe coding as code-first, refine-later prototyping. It lowers the barrier to making an app, but students should ask who understands the result, who maintains it, what bugs are hidden, and whether speed has replaced responsibility. Use this to judge readiness: what kind of model is being shown, how close it is to the final thing, where humans or assumptions are hidden, and why a prototype can both teach and mislead.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students see that easier creation is not automatically better creation. At the section level, it shows how prototypes can now appear extremely quickly in software. At the year-theme level, it connects to progress and prediction because easier tools may speed up innovation while also flooding the world with weaker products.
entries.takeaway: Easy software creation still needs quality control, maintenance, and support.
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether democratizing software creation broadens innovation or weakens reliability and trust.
entries.counterargument: Lower barriers can create low-quality software, but they can also open real experimentation and access that older systems kept artificially narrow.

items.title: Natural-language coding

entries.title: The Mechanical Turk and the Ethics of Fake Intelligence
entries.rawOfficialText: In 1770, a Hungarian engineer announced a startling invention: a machine capable of playing chess. This “Mechanical Turk” toured the world, impressing everyone from Benjamin Franklin to Edgar Allan Poe. There was just one problem: it was not an early example of AI, but a hoax, operated by a chess master hidden within. Companies today may still take a similar approach-known as “Wizard of Oz” testing-to test user response before production. Discuss with your team: is it okay to mislead users during product testing in order to make the finished versions of those products better?
entries.studentExplanation:
The Mechanical Turk looked like a machine that could play chess, but a hidden human actually controlled the moves. Modern Wizard of Oz testing is not exactly the same as a hoax: designers sometimes simulate an unfinished system so they can learn how users respond before building the full product. The ethical problem is transparency. A simulation can help design, but it can also mislead people if they believe the system is more real or more capable than it is.

The Mechanical Turk is the old version of a modern tech problem: the audience thinks it is seeing a machine, but a human operator supplies the intelligence. Wizard-of-Oz testing can be legitimate if disclosed inside research, but deceptive if it sells readiness that does not exist. Use this to judge readiness: what kind of model is being shown, how close it is to the final thing, where humans or assumptions are hidden, and why a prototype can both teach and mislead.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students separate testing, simulation, and deception. At the section level, it shows that some prototypes work by hiding what is incomplete. At the year-theme level, it connects to thresholds because a product can cross from useful simulation into unfair deception.
entries.takeaway: Simulated systems can help design, but hidden help should not create false trust.
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether controlled illusion in product testing can be justified without undermining trust or fairness.
entries.counterargument: A limited illusion may be defensible when the risk is low and the goal is clearly to learn before building a better real system.

Avoid generic justice scales unless they are clearly tied to technology or user testing. entries.title: Rigged Demos and the Performance of Readiness
entries.rawOfficialText: Elon Musk once unveiled a prototype humanoid robot that turned out to be a dancer in a costume. His more recent demonstrations are closer-but still not quite there. Read about some other examples of companies faking demonstrations of their not-quite-ready products, then discuss with your team: should companies be required to tell people when demos are slightly or entirely rigged?
entries.studentExplanation:
A demo can make a product look closer to ready than it really is. The Tesla Bot costume reveal is an extreme example: the audience saw a performance standing in for a future robot. Later robot demonstrations looked more advanced, but still raised questions about how much was autonomous and how much depended on control, staging, or careful conditions. This entry is about the performance of readiness: companies can show a future product in ways that inspire belief before the product is fully real.

Use Tesla Bot and other staged demos to separate demonstration from capability. A demo can prove vision, attract funding, or teach designers, but it can also make audiences believe a system is autonomous, safe, or close to market when it is still largely performance. Use this to judge readiness: what kind of model is being shown, how close it is to the final thing, where humans or assumptions are hidden, and why a prototype can both teach and mislead.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students understand why demos can persuade even when they are not full proof. At the section level, it shows a darker side of prototyping, where showing the future becomes performance. At the year-theme level, it connects to prediction because public demos shape what people believe is about to arrive.
entries.takeaway: Trustworthy demos should show what is real, what is staged, and what is still unfinished.
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating how much staging companies can use before a demo stops being a vision and starts becoming deception.
entries.counterargument: A demo is not the final product, so some simplification and staging may be expected as long as the audience is not misled about the real state of the technology.

items.title: Tesla Bot costume reveal
items.note: A public technology demonstration can create excitement even when the product is not actually ready.
items.imageGuidance: The agent must use the IMAGESEARCH skill first to find a real image from Tesla AI Day 2021 showing the Tesla Bot dancer in a costume. This is a specific known demo, so the agent must use the actual event image or a reliable article image, not a generic robot. Preserve the raw-content link about the dancer in a costume in the app.

items.note: Staged or controlled demonstrations are common in technology culture and can blur the line between prototype, performance, and deception.

entries.title: Humans Behind the Curtain
entries.rawOfficialText: Some companies have even sold products and services that still secretly relied on people to function properly. Amazon’s AI-powered cashiers at their “Just Walk Out” grocery stores were actually overseen by thousands of low-paid workers; Fireflies.ai makes millions a year selling automated notetaking at meetings, but recently revealed that at first their powerful AI was just the founders listening in and writing stuff down. Look for similar cases, then discuss with your team: should companies be punished for releasing successful products and services that once relied on human intervention but no longer do? Is “fake it ’til you make it” justified as long as you make it in the end?
entries.studentExplanation:
Some products are sold as automated even though humans still help them work behind the scenes. Sometimes that human help is temporary while the company builds better technology. Sometimes it becomes a trust problem because users, workers, or investors may not understand what is really happening. This entry asks whether “fake it till you make it” is a clever bridge to the future or an unfair way to sell a product that is not what it seems.

The extra notes connect Amazon Just Walk Out and early Fireflies-style support to hidden labor. The key question is not only whether a product uses AI, but where human review, annotation, correction, or manual work enters the system and whether customers are allowed to know. Use this to judge readiness: what kind of model is being shown, how close it is to the final thing, where humans or assumptions are hidden, and why a prototype can both teach and mislead.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students understand hidden labor inside supposedly automated systems. At the section level, it connects prototypes, human support, and misleading appearances. At the year-theme level, it connects to progress because some products reach the market while still depending on invisible help.
entries.takeaway: Human-supported AI needs honesty about its limits, labor, and level of automation.
examples: Fake it till you make it
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether hidden human support is a pragmatic bridge to automation or a deceptive shortcut that exploits trust and labor.
entries.counterargument: A transitional human-supported phase may help a product reach a genuinely automated future, but that defense only works if transparency and fairness are taken seriously.
